← Previous Contents Next →

Chapter 4: Atoms, Molecules, and Subatomic Particles

CHEM 139 — Open Educational Resource (2026 ed.) · CC BY 4.0

Chapter Opener: A Glass of Water and 10²⁵ Tiny Things

A typical glass of water contains about 5 × 10²⁵ molecules. If you laid those molecules end to end, the line would stretch to the Sun and back many times. Yet for almost all of human history, no one knew water had "parts" at all. The idea that matter is made of small, indivisible particles — atoms — was floated in ancient Greece, dismissed for two millennia, and then resurrected by chemistry in the early 1800s. Within a century chemists could not only believe in atoms but count them, weigh them, and even split them apart.

This chapter takes that journey in compressed form. We start with John Dalton's atomic theory in 1808 and end with the modern picture of the atom as a tiny dense nucleus surrounded by an electron cloud. Along the way we meet protons, neutrons, electrons, isotopes, and atomic masses — all of which we will use for the rest of the course.

Why this matters: Almost everything quantitative about chemistry — what a mole weighs, why bonds form, why isotopes matter for medical imaging and carbon dating — flows from the atomic picture introduced here. The weighted-average atomic mass you learn to calculate in Section 4.8 is the same number you will look up on the periodic table for every mole calculation in Chapter 8 and beyond. The proton-neutron-electron picture is the working model of matter that general chemistry, organic chemistry, biochemistry, and pre-med imaging and pre-engineering materials-characterization courses all assume you already carry. Spend the time on isotopic notation now and you will read NMR spectra, mass spectra, and clinical lab values without translation later.

Chapter Learning Outcomes

  1. State Dalton's atomic theory and identify which postulates have been modified by later evidence.
  2. Distinguish atoms from molecules; classify a molecule as an element or a compound based on its formula.
  3. Interpret and write chemical formulas for simple compounds.
  4. Describe the properties (mass, charge, location) of protons, neutrons, and electrons.
  5. Define atomic number, mass number, and isotope; write isotopic notation correctly.
  6. Calculate the atomic mass of an element from isotope abundances using a weighted average.
  7. Describe the experimental evidence (cathode-ray and gold-foil experiments) that established the nuclear model of the atom.

4.1 The Atom — Dalton's Atomic Theory

By the early 1800s, chemists had collected enough quantitative data — Lavoisier's mass conservation, Proust's law of definite proportions — to suspect that matter was discrete. In 1808 the English schoolteacher John Dalton synthesized the data into a single coherent theory. Dalton's atomic theory rests on four postulates.

Table 4.1 — Dalton's atomic postulates and modern status.

#

Original postulate

Modern status

1

All matter is composed of tiny indivisible particles called atoms.

RETAINED in modified form. Atoms are NOT indivisible — they have internal structure (protons, neutrons, electrons) — but in chemical reactions they behave as the smallest unit of an element.

2

All atoms of a given element are identical in mass and properties.

MODIFIED. Atoms of the same element can have different numbers of neutrons (isotopes), giving them slightly different masses, while sharing the same chemical behavior.

3

Atoms of different elements have different masses and properties.

RETAINED.

4

Compounds form by the combination of atoms in small whole-number ratios.

RETAINED. (We now express the ratios with chemical formulas.)

5

Chemical reactions involve only the rearrangement of atoms; atoms are neither created nor destroyed.

RETAINED. (Nuclear reactions can change one element into another, but those are NOT chemical reactions.)

Source: Original postulates from Dalton (1808); modern status reflects the consensus picture circa 2025.

HISTORICAL VIGNETTE — John Dalton (1766–1844): Born to a Quaker weaver's family in northern England, Dalton was largely self-taught. By 25 he was lecturing on natural philosophy in Manchester. Color blind himself, he produced one of the first systematic studies of color blindness — still sometimes called Daltonism. His atomic theory was developed in the early 1800s and published in his New System of Chemical Philosophy (1808). Dalton drew his atoms as little wooden balls with hooks; his sketches survive in museums. He never married, never traveled abroad, and kept meticulous weather records every day for 57 years. He is buried in Manchester.

4.2 The Molecule

A molecule is a stable, electrically neutral group of two or more atoms held together by chemical bonds. The atoms in a molecule may all be the same element (then the molecule is an elemental molecule) or they may be different elements (then it is a compound molecule). Diatomic oxygen O₂, ozone O₃, and nitrogen N₂ are elemental molecules. Water H₂O, methane CH₄, and glucose C₆H₁₂O₆ are compound molecules.

Most elements at room temperature consist of single atoms (Cu, Fe, Ar, He). Seven non-metallic elements are diatomic in their natural form, an indispensable list to memorize: H₂, N₂, O₂, F₂, Cl₂, Br₂, I₂.

REMINDER: The seven diatomic elements are sometimes remembered with the mnemonic "HOFBrINCl" (read "Hoff-brink-l"). The pattern on the periodic table is also memorable: H plus the seven elements making an L-shape from N down to I (taking O, F, Cl, Br on the way).

4.3 Natural and Synthetic Compounds

Compounds may exist in nature already (water, table salt, glucose, caffeine) or be synthesized in the laboratory or in industry (Teflon, aspirin, polyester). The chemical properties of a compound depend only on its molecular structure, not on its origin. "Natural vitamin C" extracted from rose hips and "synthetic vitamin C" produced industrially are the same compound (ascorbic acid, C₆H₈O₆) and behave identically inside the body. Chemistry does not respect the marketing distinction between natural and synthetic; it only cares about which atoms are bonded to which.

4.4 Chemical Formulas

A chemical formula is a shorthand notation that tells you which elements are present in a compound and in what numerical ratio. Three reading rules:

  1. Each capital letter (with or without a following lowercase) names one element.
  2. A subscript after an element symbol gives the number of atoms of that element in one formula unit. No subscript means 1.
  3. Parentheses group a unit; a subscript outside the parentheses multiplies every atom inside.

Examples: H₂O has 2 H atoms and 1 O atom per molecule. C₆H₁₂O₆ has 6 C, 12 H, 6 O. Ca(NO₃)₂ has 1 Ca, 2×1 = 2 N atoms, and 2×3 = 6 O atoms — total 9 atoms per formula unit. Al₂(SO₄)₃ has 2 Al, 3×1 = 3 S, 3×4 = 12 O atoms.

EXAMPLE 4.1: Reading a chemical formula

How many atoms of each element are in one formula unit of (NH₄)₂CO₃?

Step 1 — The parentheses contain NH₄. The subscript 2 outside the parentheses multiplies every atom inside.

Step 2 — N: 1 × 2 = 2. H: 4 × 2 = 8. C: 1 (no subscript). O: 3.

Step 3 — Total: 2 + 8 + 1 + 3 = 14 atoms.

Show solution

2 N, 8 H, 1 C, 3 O.

4.5 Subatomic Particles: Protons, Neutrons, Electrons

By the early 1900s, experiments had shown that the "indivisible" atom in fact contains three kinds of subatomic particles: protons, neutrons, and electrons. Modern atoms are arranged with a tiny dense nucleus (containing all the protons and all the neutrons) surrounded by a cloud of electrons that occupies the rest of the atom's volume.

Table 4.2 — Properties of the three main subatomic particles.

Particle

Symbol

Charge

Mass (amu)

Mass (kg)

Location

Proton

p, p⁺

+1

1.00728

1.673 × 10⁻²⁷

Nucleus

Neutron

n, n⁰

0

1.00867

1.675 × 10⁻²⁷

Nucleus

Electron

e, e⁻

−1

0.000549

9.109 × 10⁻³¹

Outside the nucleus (electron cloud)

Source: CODATA 2018 recommended values; "amu" denotes the unified atomic mass unit (u).

KEY FACTS: • Protons and neutrons have nearly equal mass (≈ 1 amu); the electron is about 1/1836 the mass of a proton. • Atoms are electrically neutral when the number of electrons equals the number of protons. • If electrons are gained or lost, the atom becomes a charged ion (Chapter 6). • The nucleus is roughly 100,000 times smaller than the atom — most of the atom is empty space.

Adapted from OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Figure 2.11 (Flowers, Theopold, Langley, Robinson). Access for free at openstax.org. Licensed CC BY 4.0.

Figure 4.1 — Modern model of the atom (not to scale).

Alt text: Schematic of an atom with a small dense nucleus at center surrounded by a fuzzy electron cloud.

Description: A square panel ~3 inches on a side. At center, a small red sphere (~10% of width) labeled "Nucleus (protons + neutrons)." Around it, a fuzzy cloudy shaded blue region taking up most of the panel, labeled "Electron cloud." Two small white circles inside the nucleus — one labeled p⁺, the other n⁰. Outside the nucleus, three or four small dots labeled e⁻ scattered in the cloud. Below the panel: "Atom diameter ≈ 100,000 × nucleus diameter — drawing not to scale." Use Arial 12 pt for labels.

Caption: Figure 4.1 — Modern atom: a tiny dense nucleus of protons and neutrons at the center, surrounded by electrons occupying the bulk of the atom's volume.

4.6 Atomic Number and Mass Number

Two integers identify any atom uniquely:

  • Atomic number (Z) = the number of protons in the nucleus. Z determines which element the atom is. All carbon atoms have Z = 6; all gold atoms have Z = 79.
  • Mass number (A) = the number of protons plus the number of neutrons in the nucleus. A is always an integer. The number of neutrons is therefore A − Z.

In a neutral atom (no net charge), the number of electrons equals Z.

The standard isotope notation places A and Z on a single element symbol:

ᴬ_ZX (also written A-X, e.g., "carbon-14").

Examples: ¹²₆C is carbon-12 (6 protons, 6 neutrons). ¹⁴₆C is carbon-14 (6 protons, 8 neutrons). ²³₁₁Na is sodium-23 (11 p, 12 n). ²³⁵₉₂U is uranium-235 (92 p, 143 n). ²³⁸₉₂U is uranium-238 (92 p, 146 n). ¹⁶O₈ → ¹⁶₈O is oxygen-16 (8 p, 8 n).

4.7 Isotopes

Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same Z) that differ in the number of neutrons (so different A). All carbon atoms have 6 protons. Most have 6 neutrons (¹²C, ~98.9% of natural carbon); a small fraction have 7 neutrons (¹³C, ~1.1%); a vanishing fraction have 8 neutrons (¹⁴C, present in trace amounts and used for radiocarbon dating). All three are carbon and behave nearly identically in chemical reactions; they differ only in mass and (for ¹⁴C) in radioactive stability.

4.8 Atomic Masses

The atomic mass of an element printed on the periodic table is not a whole number. Carbon's atomic mass is 12.011, not 12. The reason is that the periodic-table value is a weighted average over all naturally occurring isotopes.

The unified atomic mass unit (u, also called the dalton, Da) is defined so that one ¹²C atom has a mass of exactly 12 u. Other atomic masses are measured relative to this standard, originally by classical chemistry, today by mass spectrometry. A mass spectrometer ionizes atoms and uses electric and magnetic fields to separate them by mass-to-charge ratio; the abundance of each isotope is read from the resulting spectrum.

Calculating an Atomic Mass from Isotope Abundances

Atomic mass = Σ (isotope mass × fractional abundance), where the sum runs over all naturally occurring isotopes and fractional abundance = percent abundance ÷ 100.

EXAMPLE 4.2: Carbon's atomic mass from isotope data

Natural carbon contains 98.93% ¹²C (mass 12.0000 u, by definition) and 1.07% ¹³C (mass 13.00335 u). Calculate the atomic mass.

Step 1 — Convert percent to fraction: 0.9893 and 0.0107.

Step 2 — Atomic mass = (12.0000 × 0.9893) + (13.00335 × 0.0107).

Step 3 — = 11.8716 + 0.1391 = 12.0107.

Step 4 — Sig figs: input data have 4–5 sig figs; the answer is reported as 12.011.

Show solution

12.011 u (matches periodic-table value).

EXAMPLE 4.3: Chlorine's atomic mass

Natural chlorine is 75.78% ³⁵Cl (34.969 u) and 24.22% ³⁷Cl (36.966 u). Atomic mass?

Step 1 — Fractions: 0.7578 and 0.2422.

Step 2 — = (34.969 × 0.7578) + (36.966 × 0.2422) = 26.50 + 8.95 = 35.45 u.

Show solution

35.45 u (matches periodic-table value).

EXAMPLE 4.4: Boron's atomic mass

Boron occurs as 19.9% ¹⁰B (10.013 u) and 80.1% ¹¹B (11.009 u). Atomic mass?

Step 1 — = (10.013 × 0.199) + (11.009 × 0.801) = 1.993 + 8.818 = 10.811 u.

Step 2 — Round to 4 sig figs: 10.81.

Show solution

10.81 u.

4.9 Evidence for Subatomic Particles

Cathode-Ray Experiments — Discovering the Electron

In the late 1800s, J. J. Thomson worked with cathode-ray tubes — sealed glass tubes from which most of the air had been pumped out, with a metal plate at each end connected to a high-voltage source. When the voltage was turned on, a faint glow appeared moving from the negative plate (cathode) toward the positive plate. Thomson showed that this beam was deflected by both electric and magnetic fields and always carried a negative charge, regardless of the metal used to make the cathode. He concluded — radically, in 1897 — that the beam was a stream of negatively charged particles smaller than any atom. The electron had been discovered.

Gold-Foil Experiments — Discovering the Nucleus

In 1909, Hans Geiger and Ernest Marsden, working in Ernest Rutherford's lab, fired alpha particles (small, fast, positively charged particles) at a thin sheet of gold foil. The dominant model of the atom at the time was Thomson's "plum pudding": a diffuse positive cloud sprinkled with electrons. That model predicted that all the alpha particles should pass through the foil with at most slight deflections.

They mostly did. But about 1 in every 8000 alpha particles bounced sharply backward, as if it had hit something solid. Rutherford famously remarked that it was "almost as if you fired a 15-inch shell at a piece of tissue paper and it came back and hit you." The result forced a new model: the atom must have a small, dense, positively charged core — the nucleus — that occasionally deflects an alpha particle by direct collision. The remainder of the atom must be mostly empty space, populated only by electrons.

Adapted from OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Figure 2.9 (Flowers, Theopold, Langley, Robinson). Access for free at openstax.org. Licensed CC BY 4.0.

Figure 4.2 — Rutherford gold-foil experiment.

Alt text: Schematic showing alpha particles striking a thin sheet of gold foil and being scattered in three patterns: most pass straight through, some deflect, a few bounce backward.

Description: Side-view schematic. Left: a small lead box labeled "α source" emits a horizontal beam of arrows toward the right. The beam strikes a thin gold rectangle (vertical, gold-colored) labeled "Gold foil ≈ 0.0004 mm thick." Right of the foil: most alpha-particle arrows continue straight (label: "~99.99% pass through"); a few branch off at small angles (label: "some are deflected by passing near a nucleus"); one or two arrows bounce backward toward the left side of the foil (label: "~1 in 8000 bounces back from direct nuclear collision"). Surrounding the system: a circular zinc sulfide screen with flashes at points where particles strike. Use Arial 12 pt for labels and a vertical scale arrow showing "~5 cm" for the foil-to-screen distance.

Caption: Figure 4.2 — The unexpected backscatter of a small fraction of alpha particles told Rutherford that an atom must have a tiny, dense, positively charged nucleus.

HISTORICAL VIGNETTE — Ernest Rutherford (1871–1937): Rutherford, born on a New Zealand farm, won the 1908 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for his work on radioactivity — and was reportedly amused to be a chemistry laureate, since he considered himself a physicist. His lab in Manchester, where the gold-foil experiment was performed, also trained Niels Bohr, James Chadwick (who later discovered the neutron), and Ernest Walton (who first split the atomic nucleus). Rutherford's quip about the alpha particles bouncing back is now textbook lore. His ashes lie next to Isaac Newton's in Westminster Abbey.

Practice Problems by Topic

Dalton, Atoms, and Molecules

Subatomic Particles

Atomic Number, Mass Number, Isotopes

Atomic Mass Calculations

Evidence for Subatomic Particles

1. Which of Dalton's postulates was modified by the discovery of isotopes?

Show solution

Postulate 2 — atoms of the same element are NOT exactly identical in mass; isotopes have different neutron counts.

2. Classify as elemental molecule or compound molecule:

  1. O₃,
  2. NH₃,
  3. S₈,
  4. HCl,
  5. P₄.
Show solution

Elemental: a, c, e. Compound: b, d.

3. List the seven diatomic elements.

Show solution

H₂, N₂, O₂, F₂, Cl₂, Br₂, I₂.

4. How many atoms total are in one formula unit of Mg(OH)₂?

Show solution

Mg: 1; O: 2; H: 2; total 5 atoms.

5. Count atoms of each element in C₂H₅OH.

Show solution

C: 2; H: 6 (5 + 1); O: 1.

6. Count atoms in (NH₄)₃PO₄.

Show solution

N: 3; H: 12; P: 1; O: 4. Total: 20.

7. Order the three subatomic particles by mass, smallest to largest.

Show solution

Electron < proton ≈ neutron (proton and neutron are nearly equal; neutron is very slightly heavier).

8. Which subatomic particle has no charge?

Show solution

Neutron.

9. Where in an atom are protons and neutrons located?

Show solution

In the nucleus.

10. Why is most of an atom "empty space"?

Show solution

Electrons occupy a large diffuse volume around a tiny dense nucleus; the volume of the nucleus is about 10⁻¹⁵ that of the atom.

11. An atom has Z = 17 and A = 35. Identify the element and number of neutrons.

Show solution

Cl (chlorine); n = 35 − 17 = 18 neutrons.

12. Write the isotope notation for an atom with 26 protons and 30 neutrons.

Show solution

⁵⁶₂₆Fe (iron-56).

13. An atom has 19 protons, 20 neutrons, 19 electrons. Identify and give A and Z.

Show solution

K (potassium); Z = 19; A = 39.

14. How many neutrons in ²³⁸₉₂U?

Show solution

238 − 92 = 146.

15. How many electrons in a neutral ⁴⁰₂₀Ca atom? In a Ca²⁺ ion (loss of 2 electrons)?

Show solution

Neutral: 20 electrons. Ca²⁺: 18 electrons.

16. Magnesium has three stable isotopes: ²⁴Mg (78.99%, 23.985 u), ²⁵Mg (10.00%, 24.986 u), ²⁶Mg (11.01%, 25.983 u). Calculate the atomic mass.

Show solution

(23.985×0.7899) + (24.986×0.1000) + (25.983×0.1101) = 18.94 + 2.499 + 2.861 = 24.30 u.

17. Lithium has two stable isotopes: ⁶Li (7.59%, 6.015 u) and ⁷Li (92.41%, 7.016 u). Atomic mass?

Show solution

(6.015×0.0759) + (7.016×0.9241) = 0.4565 + 6.483 = 6.940 u → 6.94 u.

18. Bromine has two stable isotopes: ⁷⁹Br (50.69%, 78.918 u) and ⁸¹Br (49.31%, 80.916 u). Atomic mass?

Show solution

(78.918×0.5069) + (80.916×0.4931) = 40.00 + 39.90 = 79.90 u.

19. Why is the atomic mass of bromine close to 80, despite both isotopes being odd-numbered (79 and 81)?

Show solution

The two isotopes have nearly equal abundance, so the weighted average lies near the midpoint.

20. Without doing the math, would the atomic mass of an element be closer to the mass of its most abundant isotope or to the average of all its isotopes? Explain.

Show solution

Closer to the most abundant isotope, since that isotope is multiplied by the largest fractional abundance.

21. Whose experiments provided the first direct evidence of the electron?

Show solution

J. J. Thomson, using cathode-ray tubes (1897).

22. What unexpected result in the gold-foil experiment forced a new model of the atom?

Show solution

About 1 in 8000 alpha particles bounced backward — a result inconsistent with a diffuse positive cloud (Thomson's plum-pudding model).

23. What conclusion did Rutherford draw from the gold-foil experiment?

Show solution

The atom contains a tiny dense positive nucleus surrounded by mostly empty space populated by electrons.

24. Why did Thomson conclude that cathode rays carried negative charge?

Show solution

They were deflected toward the positive plate of an external electric field.

25. If Rutherford had used silver foil instead of gold, what (if anything) would change?

Show solution

The general result (a small fraction of alpha particles bouncing back) would still hold; the precise scattering angles depend on nuclear charge, but the qualitative discovery of the nucleus would be the same.

Additional Practice Problems by Topic

Dalton, Atoms, and Molecules

1. State the four central postulates of Dalton’s atomic theory.

Show solution

(1) All matter is composed of atoms. (2) Atoms of a given element are identical in mass and properties. (3) Compounds are formed by combining atoms in fixed whole-number ratios. (4) Atoms are rearranged but neither created nor destroyed in chemical reactions.

2. Which of Dalton’s postulates was modified by the discovery of isotopes, and how?

Show solution

Postulate 2: atoms of the same element can have slightly different masses (isotopes), though they share chemical properties.

3. A compound contains 40.0% S and 60.0% O by mass. Express the mass ratio of S to O in lowest whole-number terms (illustrating the law of definite proportions).

Show solution

40.0/60.0 = 2/3, so the S:O mass ratio is 2:3 in any sample of this compound.

4. Two oxides of nitrogen contain 1.00 g N combined with 1.14 g O and 2.28 g O respectively. State the law illustrated.

Show solution

The masses of O combining with a fixed mass of N are in the ratio 1.14:2.28 = 1:2 — the law of multiple proportions.

5. Distinguish an atom from a molecule, and give one molecular form of an element.

Show solution

An atom is the smallest unit of an element. A molecule is two or more atoms bonded covalently. Examples of molecular elements: O₂, N₂, H₂, P₄, S₈.

Subatomic Particles

1. State the relative charge and approximate relative mass of the proton, neutron, and electron.

Show solution

Proton: +1, mass ≈ 1 u. Neutron: 0, mass ≈ 1 u. Electron: −1, mass ≈ 1/1836 u (negligible compared with nucleons).

2. In a neutral atom, what is the relationship between the number of protons and the number of electrons?

Show solution

They are equal. The atom has no net charge because the +1 charge of each proton is balanced by a −1 electron.

3. How many protons, neutrons, and electrons are in a neutral atom of ⁸¹Br?

Show solution

Z = 35 → 35 protons, 35 electrons. A = 81 → N = 81 − 35 = 46 neutrons.

4. How many protons, neutrons, and electrons are in a neutral ⁶⁰Co atom?

Show solution

Z = 27 → 27 protons, 27 electrons. N = 60 − 27 = 33 neutrons.

5. Why is the atom’s mass nearly all in the nucleus?

Show solution

The proton and neutron each have ~1 u, while the electron has ~1/1836 u. With Z protons and (A−Z) neutrons in the nucleus and only Z electrons outside, the nuclear mass overwhelmingly dominates.

Atomic Number, Mass Number, Isotopes

1. Define atomic number and mass number.

Show solution

Atomic number Z = number of protons in the nucleus (and identity of the element). Mass number A = total nucleons = Z + N (number of protons + neutrons).

2. Two nuclides have Z = 6 and A values 12 and 14. What is their relationship?

Show solution

Both are carbon (Z = 6). ¹²C and ¹⁴C are isotopes of carbon, differing in neutron count (6 vs 8).

3. Write the isotope symbol for an atom with 17 protons and 20 neutrons.

Show solution

Z = 17 (Cl), A = 37 → ³⁷Cl (or Cl-37).

4. How many neutrons are in ²³⁸U?

Show solution

A − Z = 238 − 92 = 146 neutrons.

5. Two atoms have the same Z but different A. Compare their chemical and physical properties.

Show solution

Same chemical behavior (same proton/electron count). Slightly different physical properties (mass, density, diffusion rate); for radioactive isotopes, also different nuclear stability.

Atomic Mass Calculations

1. Element X has two isotopes: X-10 (10.013 u, 19.9% abundance) and X-11 (11.009 u, 80.1%). Compute the standard atomic weight.

Show solution

0.199 × 10.013 + 0.801 × 11.009 = 1.993 + 8.818 = 10.81 u (the value for boron).

2. Chlorine has two isotopes, ³⁵Cl (34.969 u) and ³⁷Cl (36.966 u), with abundances 75.78% and 24.22%. Compute the atomic weight.

Show solution

0.7578 × 34.969 + 0.2422 × 36.966 = 26.499 + 8.953 = 35.45 u.

3. Copper has two stable isotopes: ⁶³Cu (62.930 u, 69.17%) and ⁶⁵Cu (64.928 u, 30.83%). Compute the atomic weight.

Show solution

0.6917 × 62.930 + 0.3083 × 64.928 = 43.527 + 20.017 = 63.54 u.

4. If element Q has two isotopes Q-23 and Q-25 with abundances 80% and 20%, estimate the atomic weight to 2 sig figs (assume isotope masses equal mass numbers).

Show solution

0.80(23) + 0.20(25) = 18.4 + 5.0 = 23.4 → 23 u.

5. The atomic weight of Mg is 24.31 u. Its three isotopes have masses 23.985 u, 24.986 u, and 25.983 u. The lightest is the most abundant. Why is the atomic weight closer to 24 than to 26?

Show solution

The atomic weight is the abundance-weighted mean of isotope masses. With ²⁴Mg the most abundant, the mean lies closer to its mass (24.0).

Evidence for Subatomic Particles

1. Briefly describe what cathode-ray-tube experiments demonstrated about the electron.

Show solution

Cathode rays (1) traveled in straight lines, (2) carried negative charge (deflected by electric and magnetic fields), and (3) were the same regardless of the cathode metal — implying electrons are a universal subatomic particle.

2. What did Millikan’s oil-drop experiment determine?

Show solution

The charge of the electron (the elementary charge, e ≈ 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C) and, combined with Thomson’s e/m, the electron mass.

3. Summarize the unexpected observation in the Geiger–Marsden gold-foil experiment and what Rutherford concluded from it.

Show solution

A small fraction of α particles scattered through very large angles, occasionally back toward the source. Rutherford concluded the atom’s positive charge and most of its mass are concentrated in a tiny dense nucleus.

4. Before the gold-foil experiment, what model of the atom was widely accepted, and how did the new evidence force its replacement?

Show solution

Thomson’s “plum-pudding” model (electrons embedded in a diffuse positive sphere). It could not explain the rare large-angle scattering; only a concentrated nucleus could.

5. How did the discovery of the neutron resolve a problem with the proton-only nuclear model?

Show solution

Atomic masses were greater than the number of protons would account for, and many isotopes existed. A neutral particle of comparable mass (the neutron) explained both.

Multi-Concept Problems

1. An atom has 17 protons, 18 neutrons, and 18 electrons.

  1. Identify the element.
  2. Is this atom a neutral atom or an ion?
  3. Write the symbol with charge.
Show solution
  1. Z = 17 = Cl.
  2. Electrons (18) ≠ protons (17), so it is an ion.
  3. Net charge = +17 − 18 = −1; so the species is ³⁵Cl⁻.

2. Chlorine's atomic mass is 35.45. The two stable isotopes are ³⁵Cl (mass 34.969) and ³⁷Cl (mass 36.966). Set up an algebra problem to find the percent abundances and solve.

Show solution

Let x = fractional abundance of ³⁵Cl, then 1 − x = fractional abundance of ³⁷Cl. (34.969 × x) + (36.966 × (1 − x)) = 35.45 → 34.969x + 36.966 − 36.966x = 35.45 → −1.997x = −1.516 → x = 0.7591 ≈ 75.91%. ³⁷Cl: 24.09%.

3. An atom of element X has A = 27 and 14 neutrons.

  1. Identify X.
  2. How many electrons does a neutral X atom have?
  3. Write a chemical formula for the simplest oxide of X (assume X²⁺ and O²⁻).
Show solution
  1. Z = 27 − 14 = 13 → Al.
  2. Neutral Al has 13 electrons.
  3. Al³⁺ in fact (most common); simplest oxide Al₂O₃.

4. A copper sample contains the two natural isotopes: ⁶³Cu (62.929 u, 69.17%) and ⁶⁵Cu (64.928 u, 30.83%).

  1. Calculate the atomic mass.
  2. The actual periodic-table value is 63.546. Compare and comment on percent error.
Show solution
  1. (62.929 × 0.6917) + (64.928 × 0.3083) = 43.530 + 20.017 = 63.547 u.
  2. % error = |63.547 − 63.546|/63.546 × 100 = 0.002% — within rounding.

5. One isotope of an element has 50 protons and 70 neutrons; another has 50 protons and 68 neutrons.

  1. Are they isotopes of the same element? Justify.
  2. What are their mass numbers?
  3. Identify the element and write both isotope notations.
Show solution
  1. Yes — same Z = 50 = Sn (tin).
  2. A = 120 and 118.
  3. ¹²⁰₅₀Sn and ¹¹⁸₅₀Sn.

Multiple Choice Practice Test

1. Which postulate of Dalton's theory has been MODIFIED?

(A) atoms combine in whole-number ratios

(B) all atoms of an element are identical

(C) mass is conserved in reactions

(D) atoms rearrange in reactions

2. Which subatomic particle has the smallest mass?

(A) proton

(B) neutron

(C) electron

(D) alpha particle

3. Which subatomic particle has no charge?

(A) proton

(B) neutron

(C) electron

(D) H⁺

4. Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons are called:

(A) allotropes

(B) isobars

(C) isotopes

(D) ions

5. Number of neutrons in ²³⁸₉₂U:

(A) 92

(B) 146

(C) 238

(D) 330

6. An atom has 11 protons, 12 neutrons. Mass number is:

(A) 11

(B) 12

(C) 23

(D) 33

7. Diatomic at room temperature:

(A) He

(B) Cl₂

(C) Cu

(D) Ne

8. Atoms in one formula unit of Ca(NO₃)₂:

(A) 6

(B) 9

(C) 11

(D) 13

9. Element X: 60.0% mass 50.0; 40.0% mass 52.0. Atomic mass:

(A) 50.0

(B) 50.8

(C) 51.0

(D) 52.0

10. Rutherford concluded the nucleus is:

(A) spread throughout

(B) tiny and dense at center

(C) the same size as the atom

(D) made only of electrons

Attempt all questions before opening the answer key.

Show Answer Key and Rationales

Answer Key: 1-B 2-C 3-B 4-C 5-B 6-C 7-B 8-B 9-B 10-B

Answer Key — Worked Rationales

1. B. Dalton's postulate that all atoms of a given element are identical was modified once isotopes were discovered: same Z, different N.

2. C. Electron mass ≈ 1/1836 of a proton — by far the smallest of the three subatomic particles.

3. B. The neutron carries no net charge; protons are +1, electrons are −1.

4. C. Atoms with the same Z but different N are isotopes (same element, different mass).

5. B. ²³⁸U has Z = 92, A = 238 → N = A − Z = 146.

6. C. Mass number A = protons + neutrons = 11 + 12 = 23 (²³Na).

7. B. Cl₂ is one of the seven diatomic elements (H₂, N₂, O₂, F₂, Cl₂, Br₂, I₂). He, Cu, Ne are not diatomic.

8. B. Ca(NO₃)₂: 1 Ca + 2(N + 3 O) = 1 + 2 + 6 = 9 atoms.

9. B. Atomic mass = 0.600(50.0) + 0.400(52.0) = 30.0 + 20.8 = 50.8 u.

10. B. Rutherford's gold-foil scattering showed the positive charge and almost all the mass concentrated in a tiny dense nucleus.

Concepts to Remember

  1. Dalton's atomic theory (1808) gave chemistry the modern picture of atoms as the building blocks of matter; later evidence required modifications (atoms have substructure; isotopes exist).
  2. A molecule is two or more atoms bonded together. Elemental molecules contain a single element (O₂, N₂); compound molecules contain two or more.
  3. Seven diatomic elements: H₂, N₂, O₂, F₂, Cl₂, Br₂, I₂.
  4. Chemical formulas use subscripts to give atom ratios; parentheses with subscripts multiply through every atom inside.
  5. Atoms contain three principal subatomic particles: protons (+1, ~1 amu), neutrons (0, ~1 amu), electrons (−1, ~1/1836 amu).
  6. Atomic number Z = number of protons; defines the element.
  7. Mass number A = protons + neutrons; depends on the isotope.
  8. Isotopes share Z and chemistry, differ in A and mass.
  9. Atomic mass on the periodic table is a weighted average of isotope masses by natural abundance.
  10. Cathode-ray experiments (Thomson) discovered the electron; gold-foil experiments (Rutherford, Geiger, Marsden) discovered the nucleus.

Key Terms Glossary

Atom (4.1) — The smallest unit of an element that retains the chemical properties of that element.

Atomic mass (4.8) — The weighted average mass of an element's atoms over its naturally occurring isotopes, in unified atomic mass units (u).

Atomic number (Z) (4.6) — The number of protons in the nucleus of an atom; defines the element.

Atomic mass unit (u or amu) (4.8) — A mass unit defined so that one ¹²C atom has a mass of exactly 12 u.

Cathode-ray tube (4.9) — A sealed evacuated glass tube with electrodes at each end; the apparatus used by Thomson to discover the electron.

Chemical formula (4.4) — A symbolic representation of a compound showing which elements are present and their atomic ratios.

Compound molecule (4.2) — A molecule containing two or more elements bonded together.

Electron (e⁻) (4.5) — A subatomic particle with charge −1 and mass ~1/1836 of a proton, located outside the nucleus.

Elemental molecule (4.2) — A molecule consisting of two or more atoms of the same element.

Gold-foil experiment (4.9) — Rutherford's experiment in which alpha particles fired at thin gold foil scattered in a way that revealed the dense atomic nucleus.

Isotope (4.7) — Atoms of the same element (same Z) that differ in the number of neutrons (different A).

Mass number (A) (4.6) — The total number of protons plus neutrons in the nucleus of an atom.

Molecule (4.2) — A stable, electrically neutral group of two or more atoms held together by chemical bonds.

Neutron (n⁰) (4.5) — A subatomic particle with no charge and mass ~1 amu, located in the nucleus.

Nucleus (4.5) — The dense, positively charged center of an atom that contains the protons and neutrons.

Proton (p⁺) (4.5) — A subatomic particle with charge +1 and mass ~1 amu, located in the nucleus.

Subatomic particle (4.5) — A particle smaller than the atom — proton, neutron, or electron in introductory chemistry.

Instructor Notes

Show Instructor Notes

Common Student Difficulties

  • Students confuse mass number (A) with atomic mass (the weighted average). A is always an integer; atomic mass usually is not.
  • Counting atoms in formulas with parentheses is the most-missed problem on early exams. Drill (NH₄)₂SO₄, Ca₃(PO₄)₂, Al₂(SO₄)₃ explicitly.
  • Students sometimes write "isotopes have different chemistry." Correct: nearly identical chemistry; only mass and (some) nuclear stability differ.
  • Weighted-average problems: students forget to convert percent to fraction. Insist on this step explicitly.

Recommended In-Class Activities

  • Bean-bag isotopes: two colors of beans of slightly different mass simulate two isotopes of an element. Students count and weigh, then compute the average.
  • Build-a-formula card sort: cards with element symbols and subscripts; students assemble formulas matching given atom counts.

Lab Connections

  • Penny-as-mass-spectrometer activity: pre-1982 (mostly Cu) and post-1982 (mostly Zn-cored) pennies are mixed; weigh and compute average mass for varying mixtures, paralleling isotope abundance calculations.

End of Chapter 4.
